coredata.dc:title: Origin of extraordinary luminescence shift in graphene quantum dots with varying excitation energy: An experimental evidence of localized sp2 carbon subdomain
coredata.dc:creator: Jang, Min-Ho
Song, Sung Ho
Ha, Hyun Dong
Seo, Tae Seok
Jeon, Seokwoo
Cho, Yong-Hoon
coredata.dc:description: We report the extraordinary photoluminescence (PL) behavior of high-quality graphene quantum dots (GQDs) prepared with a graphite intercalation compound method: the alternating PL peak shifts (redshift-blueshift-redshift) with increasing the excitation wavelength. The PL peak position is redshift in the excitation wavelength range 250–270 nm, subsequently blueshift in 270–290 nm, and again redshift in the range 290–330 nm. This unconventional optical response of the GQDs is attributed to two types of energy states: intrinsic states formed by localized sp2 carbon subdomains and extrinsic states created by various oxygen-functional groups. The PL behavior of GQDs shows different tendencies according to the size and amount of oxygen-functional groups. Furthermore, since the extrinsic states of GQDs can be inactivated under acidic condition, its extraordinary PL behavior of high-quality GQDs with varying excitation wavelength largely recedes. On the other hand, we clearly observed these alternating PL shift behaviors when localized sp2 carbon subdomains with intrinsic emission are artificially formed by reducing oxygen-functional groups in graphene oxide quantum dots, which have mostly the extrinsic PL originated from the energy states of various oxygen-functional groups. The results offer experimental evidence for the existence of localized sp2 carbon subdomains and provide key information that clarifies the electronic structure of GQDs and their derivatives.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical characterization of GQDs in acidic condition
Optical characterization of GOQDs and rGOQDs
Modeling of the PL mechanism in GQDs and their derivatives

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology
Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (2016R1A2A1A05005320) of the Ministry of Education, the Future Semiconductor Device Technology Development Program (10044735) funded by MOTIE and KSRC (Korea Semiconductor Research Consortium), and the Climate Change Research Hub of KAIST (Grant No.N01150041).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical characterization of GQDs prepared by the GIC method. PL spectra of (a) GQD-I and (b) GQD-II as a function of excitation wavelength (λex). (c) Temporal profile of time-integrated PL spectra of GQD-I excited at 300 nm and 380 nm as a function of recombination time (0–1 ns, 1–3 ns, 3–7 ns, 7–15 ns, and 15–31 ns). (d) The red shift of the PL peak energy (ΔE) in time-integrated PL spectra of GQD-I between the 0–1 ns and 15–31 ns as a function of excitation wavelength from 250 nm to 400 nm. (e) FWHM of the PL emission of GQD-I for excitation wavelengths 250–400 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Modeling of the PL mechanism in the GQDs. (a) Optical characteristics of the PL peak energy position and FWHM of GQD-I (top) and rGOQDs (bottom) divided in five regions. (b) Representation of PL spectra for different emission ratios between intrinsic and extrinsic states that correspond to the five regions. (c) Modeling of the band structure of GQD-I corresponding to PL excitation data acquired at 410 ± 10 nm (intrinsic PL) and 490 ± 10 nm (extrinsic PL). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Origin of extraordinary luminescence shift in graphene quantum dots with varying excitation energy: An experimental evidence of localized sp2 carbon subdomain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and KI for the NanoCentury, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 34141, Republic of Korea

Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Advanced Materials Engineering, Kongju National University, 56 Gongjudaehak-ro, Gongju, Chunan 32588, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 34141, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering
Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, College of Engineering, Kyung Hee University, Seochon-dong, Giheung-gu, Yongin-si, Gyeonggi-do 17140, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering and KI for the NanoCentury, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 34141, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering and KI for the NanoCentury
Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the extraordinary photoluminescence (PL) behavior of high-quality graphene quantum dots (GQDs) prepared with a graphite intercalation compound method: the alternating PL peak shifts (redshift-blueshift-redshift) with increasing the excitation wavelength. The PL peak position is redshift in the excitation wavelength range 250–270 nm, subsequently blueshift in 270–290 nm, and again redshift in the range 290–330 nm. This unconventional optical response of the GQDs is attributed to two types of energy states: intrinsic states formed by localized sp2 carbon subdomains and extrinsic states created by various oxygen-functional groups. The PL behavior of GQDs shows different tendencies according to the size and amount of oxygen-functional groups. Furthermore, since the extrinsic states of GQDs can be inactivated under acidic condition, its extraordinary PL behavior of high-quality GQDs with varying excitation wavelength largely recedes. On the other hand, we clearly observed these alternating PL shift behaviors when localized sp2 carbon subdomains with intrinsic emission are artificially formed by reducing oxygen-functional groups in graphene oxide quantum dots, which have mostly the extrinsic PL originated from the energy states of various oxygen-functional groups. The results offer experimental evidence for the existence of localized sp2 carbon subdomains and provide key information that clarifies the electronic structure of GQDs and their derivatives.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a promising research because they have the potential to be an effective luminous substance [1–3]. For instance, this material has excellent properties, such as cost-effective synthesis, good solubility, high biocompatibility, broad range of optical absorptivity, and stable photoluminescence (PL) as well as interesting luminescence properties [1,4–6]. Owing to their advantages, GQDs and their derivatives have attracted significant attention since the first report of visible luminescence from graphene in 2009 [7,8], which has been applied not only in bio-imaging and bio-sensing [3,9–13], but also in electronic and optoelectronic devices [2,14,15].

Initially, a model based on the quantum confinement effect was suggested for describing luminescence mechanism in GQDs and their derivatives [9,16,17]. However, this approach cannot explain the blue emission from graphene oxide (GO) with a size of micro-scale [9,18]. For this reason, Eda et al. proposed a new modal based on localized sp2 carbon subdomains [18]. The authors explained that GQDs and their derivatives have a number of small sp2 carbon subdomains surrounded by oxygen-functional groups; such a sp2 carbon subdomain can confine an electron-hole pair and induce visible light emission. However, the measurement of small sp2 carbon subdomains surrounded by oxygen-functional groups through imaging techniques such as scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, scanning tunneling microscopy, and atomic-force microscopy, is difficult. Nevertheless, indirect, but strong, evidence of the existence of localized sp2 carbon subdomains in GQDs has been achieved by means of optical analysis [14,18,19] and rigorous computational works [20]. For example, a distinct absorption peak and a PL excitation peak in the ultraviolet (UV) region can be observed when the GQDs and their derivatives have sp2 carbon subdomains with a high-crystallinity graphene structure [14,19,21]. In addition, in chemically reduced GO and GO quantum dots (GOQDs), the light emission from the sp2 carbon subdomains has faster recombination time than that by oxygen-functional groups [19,22]. However, although many optical features of GQDs have been reported, the electronic structure of GQDs with small sp2 carbon subdomains remains an open question.

Here, we present an extraordinary PL behavior of GQDs that constitutes a direct experimental evidence for the existence of localized sp2 carbon subdomains in GQDs and we propose a model for the luminescence of GQDs. The presence of the localized sp2 carbon subdomains manifests the optical characteristics of GQDs. As the excitation wavelength increases, the PL peak energy position of the GQDs emission shows an unconventional alternating shift behavior (redshift-blueshift-redshift), which will be referred to as ‘RBR shift’. To verify the origin of this intriguing optical property of GQDs emission, we performed two experiments: (i) we investigated the pH dependence of the GQD solvent to control the extrinsic emission originating from various oxygen-functional groups and (ii) compared GOQDs and reduced GOQDs (rGOQDs) to confirm the change in the PL spectrum after the formation of the localized sp2 carbon subdomains. The results revealed that its RBR shift behavior is present only when the GQDs have two discrete luminescent modes that originate from the localized sp2 carbon subdomains and the oxygen-functional groups. A comprehensive analysis indicated that the band structure of the localized sp2 carbon subdomains is responsible for the alternating PL peak shift depending on excitation energy.

The GQDs were fabricated using potassium sodium tartrate to synthesize graphite intercalation compounds (GICs) at low temperature. The first step of the process involved the mixing and grinding of potassium sodium tartrate (200 mg) with graphite (20 mg, SP-1, Bay Carbon Inc.) in the mass fraction ratio. Then, the homogeneous grinded mixtures reacted in an autoclave vessel at 250 °C for 24 h. The manufactured GICs were exfoliated in water and then produced well-defined GQDs. Then, the sizes of the GQDs were controlled by filtration (10,000 and 30,000 NMWL, Amicon Ultra-15) methods and dialyzed in dialysis tubing to remove the remaining salts [14].

For the fabrication of the GOQDs, graphite nano-particles were pre-oxidized in a concentrated H2SO4 (60 mL) and HNO3 (20 mL) solution for 3 h under mild ultra-sonication (100 W). The mixture was stirred at 100 °C under a refluxing condition for 12 h, and then diluted with deionized (DI) water. The suspension was centrifuged at 20,000 rpm and the supernatant was carefully separated. The supernatant was pre-dialyzed to remove acidic ions for one day and the pH was adjusted to 8 with a diluted NaOH solution. The final product was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (Molecular weight cut-off: 1000 Da) for three day to completely remove the salt ions [10,19]. The rGOQDs were prepared by adding 50 mg NaBH4 in the GOQD solution and stirring at room temperature for 3 h. The products were dialyzed in a dialysis bag (Molecular weight cut-off: 1 kDa) for one day to remove sodium ions or residues [19].

All the luminescence data were obtained from liquid based samples (DI water) using a hand-made setup with precision cells constructed from Suprasil quartz. The PL spectra were acquired using monochromatic light (from 240 nm to 400 nm) from a 300 W-Xenon lamp and UV spectrometers (Maya2000, Ocean Optics, USA) at room temperature. A time-resolved PL experiment was performed using a mode-locked femtosecond pulsed Ti:Sapphire laser (Coherent, Chameleon Ultra II) system and a streak camera detector (Hamamatsu, C7700-01). The excitation source was prepared by third-harmonic-generation (from 250 nm to 330 nm) and second-harmonic-generation (from 360 nm to 400 nm), and the pulse repetition rate was 5 MHz. The decay profile of the PL spectra was obtained at room temperature.

The GQDs used in this study were prepared by the metal salts intercalated graphite compound method. The details of the fabrication method and the basic properties of the GQDs are provided elsewhere [14]. The average diameters of the two different GQDs were 3–5 nm and 7–10 nm; these GQDs will be referred to as GQD-I and GQD-II, respectively. Both samples had similar height distributions of 0.8–1.5 nm, corresponding to 1–3 graphene layers. Based on X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, the oxygen-functional groups conjugated with surface and edge of GQDs. The ratios of oxygen to carbon in GQD-I and GQD-II were determined to be approximately 0.22 and 0.37, respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis of the GQDs show oxygen-related peaks of the CH stretch, CO/COOH stretch, and the OH stretch are observed, but the epoxide stretch is very small in the GQDs fabricated by the metal salts intercalated graphite compound method [14].

Fig. 1 clearly exhibits the excitation-wavelength-dependent PL behavior of GQD-I and GQD-II. As the excitation wavelength increases, the PL peak energy position (E
p ) of the GQD-I emission clearly shows alternating wavelength shift (redshift-blueshift-redshift), as shown in Fig. 1a. Specifically, the E
p of GQD-I decreases from 3.07 eV (403.9 nm) to 2.86 eV (433.6 nm) in the excitation wavelength range 250–270 nm, subsequently increases from 2.86 eV (433.6 nm) to 3.05 eV (406.6 nm) in 270–290 nm, and again decreases from 3.05 eV (406.6 nm) to 2.82 eV (439.7 nm) in the range 290–330 nm. When the excitation wavelengths further increase above 330 nm, the emissions are more red shift (Fig. S1). In the case of GQD-II, the E
p also shows similar result to GQD-I (Fig. 1a) but has a smaller blue shift (70 meV) than that of GQD-I (190 meV). The E
p of GQD-II decreases from 2.66 eV (466.2 nm) to 2.55 eV (486.3 nm) in the excitation wavelength range 250–290 nm, then increases from 2.55 eV (486.3 nm) to 2.62 eV (473.3 nm) in 290–320 nm, and again decreases from 2.62 eV (473.3 nm) to 2.43 eV (510.3 nm) in the range 320–400 nm (Fig. 1b). This experimental observation is unexpected compared with the commonly reported excitation-wavelength-dependent PL behavior of GQDs and their derivatives: a simple red-shift behavior of the E
p with increasing excitation wavelength, which has been generally explained by the non-uniformity of the size of the sp2 carbon subdomain and trap states formed by surface functional groups in GQDs [23,24]. Our unique observation is difficult to verify using earlier explanations. Fig. 1a and 1b show that the PL spectra for different excitation wavelengths have asymmetric or symmetric spectral shapes centered at the E
p . The reason for the asymmetry of the PL spectra is that the GQDs used in the study had two luminescent origins of the intrinsic states and extrinsic states, which were derived from the localized sp2 carbon subdomains and various oxygen-functional groups, respectively [14,19,21,22]. The PL from the intrinsic states typically exhibits a shorter emission wavelength and faster recombination than that of the extrinsic states. For this reason, we can postulate that its extraordinary PL behavior of GQDs can be attributed to the different strengths of the intrinsic states and extrinsic states in the PL spectrum depending on the excitation energy.

To elucidate carrier dynamics in the GQDs depending on the excitation energy, we measured the time-resolved PL using a femtosecond Ti:sapphire laser and a streak camera detector. Fig. 1c exhibits the representative temporal profiles of the time-integrated PL (TIPL) spectra of GQD-I for the excitation wavelengths 300 nm and 380 nm. The emission originating from intrinsic states and extrinsic states can be easily distinguished by comparing the PL decay profiles of TIPL spectrum because the intrinsic and extrinsic states have different recombination processes and times [19,21,25]. When the profiles are expressed as a function of recombination time, spectral migration is observed from 400 nm to 425 nm for an excitation with a 300 nm wavelength (upper graph in Fig. 1c). This red shift is caused by the recombination time difference in each excited state as well as the carrier transfer effect between two states [14,19]. On the other hand, for an excitation wavelength above 380 nm, the red shifted spectral migration disappears (bottom graph in Fig. 1c). The PL spectrum retains an invariable emission wavelength because only the extrinsic states are excited. With an increase in the excitation wavelength from 250 nm to 400 nm, the redshift of the PL peak energy (ΔE) between the temporal profile of TIPL for 0–1 ns and 15–31 ns are shown in Fig. 1d (details in Fig. S2, Supporting Information). The ΔE value is the highest at 280 nm excitation and gradually decreases by increasing the excitation wavelength above 280 nm. Besides, we determined that ΔE follows a similar trend to the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the PL spectra of GQD-I with varying excitation wavelength, as shown in Fig. 1d and 1e. The FWHM is the largest value at approximately 1.28 eV when irradiated by a 280 nm excitation source. The FWHM increases from 0.99 eV to 1.28 eV in the excitation wavelength range 250–280 nm; then, its value almost saturates at 0.91 eV for excitation wavelength 280–340 nm; finally, it rapidly decreases from 0.91 eV (λex = 340 nm) to 0.66 eV (λex = 400 nm). In this regard, the trends of ΔE and FWHM are related to the differences between the portions of intrinsic and extrinsic emission, which depend on the excitation wavelength. Based on the experiment results and analysis, we confirmed that the unusual optical property of GQDs appears only in a specific range of excitation energy that simultaneously covers the energy levels of both intrinsic states and extrinsic states. Hence, we investigated the excitation-wavelength-dependent PL behaviors in the following different types of GQDs: Firstly, the GQD-I dissolved in acidic solvent mostly emits intrinsic PL owing to the protonation of oxygen-functional groups [14]. Secondly, GOQDs without the intrinsic PL are prepared [10,19]. Furthermore, we also investigated rGOQDs that are partially recovered localized sp2 carbon nano-domain with graphene crystalline by removing oxygen bonding in the GOQDs. This type has both intrinsic and extrinsic PL, similarly to GQD-I in natural solvents [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of GQDs in acidic condition
ce:section.ce:para:
Generally, the oxygen-functional group is more sensitive to the pH than the pure sp2-bonding structure [26]. In particular, under acidic conditions, the oxygen-functional groups of the GQDs are protonated [14,27]. The FT-IR spectra of GQDs show various oxygen-related groups, such as the CO stretch (∼1729 cm−1), CH stretch (∼1520 cm−1), COH stretch (∼1365 cm−1), and COC stretch (∼1047 cm−1) (Fig. S3 in Supporting Information). Especially, the FT-IR of GQDs in pH 2 solvent show more apparent peak of the COH stretch compared to that of pH 7 solvent. We consider that this reason is due to the protonation of oxygen-functional groups in GQDs by H+ ion in pH 2 solvent. For this reason, the PL emission by the extrinsic states is inactive in the PL spectrum, which results in a blue shift of the E
p.
[14,27] Therefore, we would not expect the RBR shift with excitation wavelength if the extrinsic states did not produce any emission under acidic conditions. Hence, we prepared the GQDs in a solvent with acidic conditions.

Fig. 2 shows the optical characteristics of the E
p and the FWHM for GQD-I in different solvent conditions with pH 2 (red circles) and pH 7 (black squares) as a function of excitation conditions from 240 nm to 400 nm. The graphs of the E
p and the FWHM have a strong relationship. The highest value of the FWHM, approximately 1.28 eV, corresponds to irradiation by a 280 nm excitation source and is in the range of the E
p blue shift, between the excitation wavelengths 270 nm and 290 nm. This indicates that the highest FWHM is formed for a PL emission with similar portions of intrinsic and extrinsic emission. Combined with the E
p of the PL spectra, the FWHM provides important information about the strength of each PL emission (intrinsic and extrinsic states) in the entire PL spectrum. As expected, the E
p of GQD-I under pH 2 conditions (70 meV) shows a smaller blue shift than that under pH 7 conditions (190 meV). In GQD-I under pH 2 conditions, the E
p in the excitation range 265–275 nm and above 320 nm have higher energy than that under pH 7 conditions owing to the decreasing portion of extrinsic PL. On the other hand, the E
p of GQD-I under pH 2 conditions at 250 nm and 290 nm are almost identical to those obtained under pH 7 conditions because the emission of GQD-I irradiated by 250 nm and 290 nm excitation source is dominated by the intrinsic states. In addition, the FWHM for pH 2 clearly decreases compared to that for pH 7 and has small values at excitation wavelengths 250 nm and 290 nm, which correspond to high emission energy in the diagram of the E
p (Fig. 2). This experimental result confirmed that GQD-I would not show the RBR shift behavior when PL from the extrinsic states is suppressed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of GOQDs and rGOQDs
ce:section.ce:para: If this unusual optical response of GQDs is caused by the structure of localized sp2 carbon subdomains, the same optical feature should be observed in the other samples with the localized sp2 carbon subdomains. Therefore, we prepared GOQDs and rGOQDs for comparison. The GOQDs were fabricated with an exfoliation process after chemical oxidation of graphite nanoparticles [10,19]. In our previous studies [19], the GOQDs and rGOQDs have a narrow diameter distribution between 2 and 5 nm owing to the uniform size of the graphite nanoparticles with average diameters of 4–5 nm. The thickness of nanosize graphene decreased 0.9 nm of GOQDs to 0.7 nm of rGOQDs, because the oxygen-functional groups (e.g., epoxy) anchored on the basal plane of graphene are removed after chemical reduction. In addition, FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy have shown that the peak intensity from epoxide-functional group of GOQDs decreased than that of rGOQDs. The ratios of oxygen to carbon in GOQDs and rGOQDs using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy were determined to be approximately 0.69 and 0.09, respectively. In the case of optical properties, the PL emission of GOQDs mostly originated from extrinsic states formed by oxygen-functional groups, as GOQDs rarely had localized sp2 carbon subdomains [10]. On the other hand, the rGOQDs, which were prepared by chemical reduction using the sodium borohydride (NaBH4) of the GOQDs, could have a lot of localized sp2 carbon subdomains surrounded by oxygen-functional groups.

Fig. 3 displays the PL spectra of the GOQDs and rGOQDs for different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 3a, the E
p of the GOQDs is almost constant at approximately 2.38 eV (520 nm) although it exhibits a shoulder peak near 2.82 eV (440 nm). Based on the result of the GOQDs and GQD-I under pH 2 conditions (Fig. 2), we expect that if the GQDs and their derivatives have only one luminescence, the RBR shift behavior cannot be observed. On the other hand, as shown in Fig. 3b, the PL spectra of the rGOQDs exhibited the RBR shift behavior similar to the GQD-I under pH 7 conditions (Fig. 1a), because the rGOQDs have a characteristic of the localized sp2 carbon subdomains formed by the reduction process. Fig. 3c displays the optical characteristics of the E
p and FWHM of the GOQDs (green squares) and rGOQDs (blue circles) for excitation wavelengths from 250 nm to 400 nm. Overall, the E
p of the rGOQDs are larger than that of the GOQDs because the intrinsic PL, which has higher emission energy than the extrinsic PL, rapidly increases by forming localized sp2 carbon subdomains through the chemical reduction process. In the excitation wavelength range 250–320 nm, the FWHM of the GOQDs is approximately 0.9 eV owing to the influence of a shoulder emission with a peak at 2.82 eV (440 nm). For in 320–370 nm, the FWHM gradually decreases from 0.9 eV to 0.7 eV. In the case of the rGOQDs, the FWHM exhibits three crests at 260 nm, 300 nm, and 350 nm and two troughs at 280 nm and 325 nm. Similarly to the FWHM of GQD-I (Fig. 2), this shape is related to the strength of each PL emission of the intrinsic and extrinsic states in the entire PL spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modeling of the PL mechanism in GQDs and their derivatives
ce:section.ce:para:
Although the characteristics of the E
p and the FWHM from the GQD-I and rGOQDs has a different energy scale, as shown in Fig. 4 a, the PL emission can be divided into five zones. Each zone relates to the ratio between the emission strength of the intrinsic and extrinsic states. Fig. 4b shows a representation of the PL spectrum combined with different portions of the intrinsic states (blue-line) and the extrinsic states (green-line). In the first zone, where the emission is dominated by the intrinsic states, the PL spectrum has a narrow linewidth and a high-energy PL peak position. The PL spectra in the second zone have a broad linewidth and greenish emission because of the decreasing portion of intrinsic PL. Generally, the FWHM of extrinsic PL is broader than that of intrinsic PL and can increase for PL emission with a similar ratio between intrinsic and extrinsic PL. The third region shows a blue shift of the E
p and a decreasing linewidth with increasing portion of intrinsic PL. In the fourth zone, the E
p is gradually redshifted without a particular change. However, the PL linewidths of GQD-I and rGOQDs present a spike at 340 nm and 350 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4a. This is attributed to the reduction in the portion of intrinsic PL, similar to that of the second region. Lastly, the PL peak energy and linewidth in the fifth zone decrease gradually as a function of excitation wavelength. These changes of the PL emission create the unusual optical characteristics of the E
p and the FWHM with varying excitation energy. To determine the band structure of GQD-I, we plotted the PL excitation (PLE) spectra monitored at 410 ± 10 nm of intrinsic PL and 490 ± 10 nm of extrinsic PL (Fig. 4c, right). The PLE spectrum at 410 ± 10 nm shows two peaks at 255 nm (4.86 eV) and 305 nm (4.07 eV), whereas the PLE spectrum at 490 ± 10 nm exhibits a broad intensity profile with a peak at 270 nm and shoulders above 285 nm. In the PLE spectrum of 410 ± 10 nm, the two peaks correspond to the energy levels formed by the localized sp2 carbon subdomains in GQD-I. The broadening of the PLE spectrum at 410 ± 10 nm was due to the size variation of the localized sp2 carbon subdomains in the GQDs [20]. Consequently, the band structure of the GQDs was estimated as the diagram of Fig. 4c (left). Furthermore, the energy levels of the sp2-carbon subdomain may be dependent on their size and shape [20,28,29]. In GQD-II (Fig. 1b), which has a diameter larger than that of GQD-I, their discrete energy levels of the intrinsic states were estimated using the PLE spectrum of GQD-II in a pH 2 solvent; these intrinsic states had lower energy levels than those of GQD-I (Fig. S4). We expect that localized sp2-carbon subdomains in GQD-II are composed of more different size and shapes than those of GQD-I. Besides, the PL emission of GQD-II was dominated by the extrinsic states because GQD-II had a high oxygen content. For this reason, the E
p of GQD-II shows smaller variation of the E
p than that of GQD-I (Fig. 1).

Based on these results, we propose a luminescence model in the GQDs as follows. (i) The unusual optical characteristics of the E
p and the FWHM depending on the excitation energy seem to occur in GQDs owing to existence of the localized sp2 carbon subdomains. We could observe these feature in two types of GQDs prepared with different methods, including GQD-I and GQD-II (Fig. 1), and rGOQDs (Fig. 3b). On the other hand, GQD-I under pH 2 conditions (Fig. 2), which mostly exhibits intrinsic PL owing to the suppression of extrinsic PL, and the GOQDs (Fig. 3a), which have almost no localized sp2-carbon subdomains, maintain an almost constant PL peak position. (ii) Our PLE measurement shows that intrinsic PL, formed by localized sp2 carbon subdomains, increases at the specific excitation wavelengths. Therefore, the localized sp2 carbon subdomains have specific absorption energy levels (noted as Abs. #1 and #2 in Fig. 4c) with similar density of states. A discrete density of states in GQDs with nanometer-scale sizes has been indicated by many calculations [22,30–32]. However, no sufficient experimental evidence has thus far been provided to determine the energy levels in the localized sp2-carbon subdomains. In this work, we confirm the energy levels of GQDs with localized sp2-carbon subdomains using a comprehensive analysis and PL, PL excitation, and time-resolved PL measurements for various excitation sources and samples, including GQDs prepared by the graphite intercalation compound method, GOQDs, rGOQDs, and GQDs dissolved in pH 2 solvent. Our results offer experimental evidence of the existence of sp2-carbon subdomains and an explanation of the luminescent mechanisms of GQDs and their derivatives.

To the best of our knowledge, we observed for the first time an unconventional PL behavior from GQDs and their derivatives by means of the excitation energy dependence in PL peak energy position and FWHM. Especially, the PL peak energy position for decreasing the excitation energy displays an extraordinary PL peak shift (redshift-blueshift-redshift). We attribute its origin to the different weights of the intrinsic and extrinsic states at different excitation energies. For this reason, when the GQDs and their derivatives have only one luminescence origin, their extraordinary PL behavior cannot be observed. The GQD-I in pH 2 solvent conditions exhibit smaller variation of dispersive optical response than that under pH 7 solvent conditions, because the luminescence from the extrinsic states of the GQDs is inactivated under acidic conditions. In addition, the PL peak energy positions of GOQDs, which have mostly the extrinsic PL formed by oxygen-functional groups, were unchanged with different excitation energies. On the other hand, when the localized sp2 carbon subdomains formed by reducing oxygen-functional groups in GOQDs, the alternating PL peak shift (redshift-blueshift-redshift) is clearly observed as a function of the excitation energy. Finally, the PL excitation results of GQD-I determined the discrete energy levels originated from localized sp2 carbon subdomains in GQD-I. These discrete energy levels lead to the unusual optical response of the GQDs and their derivatives. Our study provides further understanding of the electronic structure and optical properties of GQDs and their derivatives.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (2016R1A2A1A05005320) of the Ministry of Education, the Future Semiconductor Device Technology Development Program (10044735) funded by MOTIE and KSRC (Korea Semiconductor Research Consortium), and the Climate Change Research Hub of KAIST (Grant No.N01150041).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.03.060.
